title: Twitter API keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Twitter Consumer API key detected. Twitter Consumer API keys (also known as API keys or Consumer Keys) are 25-character alphanumeric credentials used to authenticate applications with the Twitter API v1.1 and v2. These keys identify your application to Twitter and are used in OAuth 1.0a authentication flows along with Consumer Secrets. When a Twitter Consumer API key is compromised, attackers can impersonate your application to access Twitter's API services, consume your monthly API usage quotas and rate limits, access user data if your app has been granted permissions, post tweets on behalf of users who have authorized your compromised application, access direct messages if your app has appropriate permissions, retrieve follower lists, user profiles, and tweet data, execute expensive API operations that could exhaust your rate limits, access premium Twitter API features if you have paid subscriptions, potentially access Tweet annotations, user lookup data, and compliance data if permissions exist, and gather intelligence about your application's functionality and user base through API usage patterns. The impact extends beyond direct API abuse as compromised keys can lead to brand reputation damage through unauthorized posts, regulatory compliance issues if user data is accessed inappropriately, financial losses from wasted API quota consumption, service disruptions when rate limits are exhausted, and potential legal liability if the compromise leads to user data exposure or privacy violations. Immediately secure your Twitter API credentials by logging into the Twitter Developer Portal, navigate to your app dashboard, regenerate the compromised API key and secret pair, update all application configurations, CI/CD pipelines, and deployment environments with the new credentials, review Twitter API usage logs and analytics to identify unauthorized activity during the exposure period, check for any unauthorized tweets, follows, or direct messages sent through your application, rotate all associated Twitter API secrets including Consumer Secret and any user access tokens, implement additional monitoring for unusual API usage patterns and rate limit consumption, revoke permissions for any suspicious third-party applications, review and audit user permissions granted to your application, set up alerts for API usage anomalies and rate limit approaches, use environment variables or secure secret management systems to store Twitter API credentials, never commit credentials to version control or expose them in client-side code, implement proper OAuth 2.0 flows where possible instead of lower-security authentication methods, maintain separate Twitter developer applications and keys for development, staging, and production environments, and document this security incident for compliance and audit purposes.

Twitter Consumer Secret detected. Twitter Consumer Secrets (also known as API Secret Keys) are 50-character alphanumeric credentials that work in conjunction with Consumer Keys to authenticate applications with the Twitter API. These secrets are used in OAuth 1.0a authentication flows and provide the cryptographic foundation for secure API communication. When a Twitter Consumer Secret is compromised, attackers gain the ability to forge authentication signatures and fully impersonate your application, make unrestricted API calls on behalf of your application, access all user data your application has permissions for, post tweets, send direct messages, and interact with user accounts, consume your entire monthly API usage allowance and rate limits, access premium Twitter API features and paid tiers if subscribed, execute write operations including posting, following, unfollowing, and account modifications, retrieve sensitive user data including email addresses, phone numbers, and private profile information if permissions exist, access compliance and enterprise API endpoints if your application has those privileges, potentially access historical tweet data, user analytics, and audience insights through premium endpoints, exhaust expensive API operations that could cost significant money with premium Twitter API plans, and reverse-engineer your application's functionality and authentication flows. The security implications are severe because Consumer Secrets enable complete application impersonation without any additional authentication factors, making this one of the most critical Twitter API credentials to protect. The business impact includes brand reputation damage from unauthorized social media activity, regulatory compliance violations if user personal data is accessed, financial losses from API quota consumption and potential premium feature abuse, service disruptions when rate limits are reached, legal liability for privacy violations or unauthorized user data access, loss of user trust if accounts are compromised through your application, and potential suspension of your Twitter Developer account for policy violations. Take immediate action by logging into the Twitter Developer Portal, accessing your application dashboard, regenerating both the Consumer Key and Consumer Secret as they work as a pair, updating all application code, configuration files, environment variables, and deployment systems with the new credentials, conducting a thorough review of Twitter API access logs to identify any unauthorized activity during the compromise window, checking for unauthorized tweets, follows, direct messages, or account modifications made through your application, rotating any associated user access tokens if they might be compromised, auditing all user permissions and revoking access for any suspicious accounts, implementing enhanced monitoring for API usage patterns and authentication anomalies, setting up alerts for unusual rate limit consumption or API endpoint access, reviewing Twitter webhook configurations and revoking any unauthorized subscriptions, strengthening credential storage using secure environment variables or dedicated secret management services, implementing proper OAuth 2.0 PKCE flows where applicable for enhanced security, using separate Twitter applications and credential sets for development, staging, and production environments, enabling Twitter's additional security features like IP address restrictions if available, conducting a security audit of your codebase to ensure no credentials are exposed in logs or error messages, and maintaining detailed incident documentation for security audits and compliance reporting.

Twitter Bearer Token detected. Twitter Bearer Tokens are OAuth 2.0 credentials that start with 'AAAAA' followed by a base64-encoded string and provide application-only authentication for accessing public Twitter data without user context. These tokens enable read-only access to public tweets, user profiles, trends, and other publicly available Twitter content through the Twitter API v1.1 and v2. When a Twitter Bearer Token is compromised, attackers can consume your application's rate limits for public endpoints, access public tweet data, user profiles, and timeline information, retrieve trending topics, search results, and public list data, exhaust your monthly API usage quotas on free or paid Twitter API plans, execute expensive searches and data retrieval operations, gather competitive intelligence about trending content and public conversations, access Twitter API v2 endpoints for public data if your application has those permissions, consume premium search and historical data access if subscribed to paid Twitter API tiers, and potentially identify your application's data collection patterns and research interests through API usage analysis. While Bearer Tokens cannot post content or access private user data directly, they enable significant data harvesting capabilities that can impact your API quotas and research activities. The business risks include exceeded API rate limits preventing your legitimate application from functioning, unexpected costs from premium Twitter API feature consumption, competitive disadvantage if proprietary research queries are exposed through API logs, intellectual property concerns if your data collection strategies become visible, service disruptions when API quotas are exhausted during critical operations, and potential Twitter Developer Account policy violations if the token is used for prohibited activities like excessive automation or data scraping. Immediately protect your Twitter API access by logging into the Twitter Developer Portal, navigating to your application dashboard under 'Projects & Apps', regenerating the compromised Bearer Token, updating all application configurations, scripts, CI/CD pipelines, and deployment systems with the new token, analyzing Twitter API usage logs during the exposure period to identify unauthorized data access patterns, checking for unusual spikes in API call volume or unexpected endpoint usage, reviewing your Twitter API billing for any surprising premium feature consumption, implementing monitoring and alerting for abnormal API usage patterns and rate limit approaches, rotating any related authentication tokens if they share the same security boundary, strengthening token storage using secure environment variables, encrypted configuration files, or dedicated secret management systems like AWS Secrets Manager or Azure Key Vault, implementing IP address restrictions if supported by Twitter's API access controls, setting up usage quotas and rate limiting within your application to prevent token abuse, using separate Bearer Tokens for different environments (development, testing, production) and use cases (research, monitoring, analytics), enabling detailed API request logging to detect and respond to suspicious activity quickly, reviewing Twitter's API usage policies to ensure compliance, and maintaining comprehensive incident documentation for security audits and compliance requirements.

Twitter API credential detected in assignment. This pattern identifies Twitter API credentials (Consumer Keys, Consumer Secrets, or Bearer Tokens) when they are assigned to variables or used in configuration settings. Twitter API credentials provide authenticated access to Twitter's social media platform APIs for posting tweets, reading user data, accessing direct messages, managing followers, and retrieving analytics. When these credentials are compromised through hardcoded values in source code, attackers can fully impersonate your Twitter application to post unauthorized content to user accounts, send direct messages and spam, access private user data including email addresses and phone numbers if permissions exist, follow/unfollow accounts to manipulate follower metrics, consume API rate limits preventing legitimate application functionality, access premium Twitter API features and paid tiers, execute expensive API operations that drain usage quotas, retrieve competitor intelligence through searches and user data access, manipulate trending topics through coordinated posting, access Twitter analytics and audience insights for business intelligence gathering, and potentially violate Twitter's terms of service leading to application suspension. The reputational damage from compromised Twitter credentials can be severe, including brand damage from unauthorized posts or offensive content, loss of user trust and follower engagement, regulatory scrutiny if user data privacy is compromised, financial losses from API quota consumption and premium feature abuse, legal liability for privacy violations or spam activity, competitive disadvantage from exposed social media strategies, service disruptions when rate limits are exceeded, and potential permanent suspension of Twitter Developer accounts and associated social media presence. Immediately secure your Twitter integration by logging into the Twitter Developer Portal, regenerating all exposed API credentials including Consumer Keys, Consumer Secrets, and Bearer Tokens, updating all application code, configuration files, environment variables, and deployment systems, analyzing Twitter API usage logs to identify unauthorized activity during the exposure window, checking user accounts for unauthorized posts, follows, or direct messages, revoking permissions for any suspicious third-party applications connected to user accounts, rotating user access tokens if they may have been compromised, implementing enhanced monitoring for API usage anomalies and social media activity, setting up alerts for unusual posting patterns or rate limit consumption, reviewing Twitter webhook configurations and disabling any unauthorized subscriptions, strengthening credential management using secure environment variables or dedicated secret management solutions, implementing proper OAuth flows with minimal required permissions, using separate Twitter applications and credentials for different environments and purposes, enabling available security features like IP restrictions and access controls, conducting a comprehensive security audit to identify and eliminate any other exposed credentials, and maintaining detailed incident documentation for security reviews and compliance reporting.
